entries.title: About the Course
entries.content:
This course includes the following topic(s): New Testament: Grades 10-11, Church History: Grades 10-12, Spiritual Formation: Grades 10-11

About Old Testament: Grades 10-12
Read the OT chronologically and study NT epistles. Students use commentaries/guides to understand cultural, historical, and theological contexts. Students also read books on particular topics to help them grow spiritually and study Church History connected to the time period., Read the OT chronologically and study NT epistles.  Grade 12 also studies Psalms. Students use commentaries/guides to understand cultural, historical, and theological contexts. Students also read books on particular topics to help them grow spiritually and study Church History connected to the time period.

About New Testament: Grades 10-11
Each year in High School, students read one of the gospels along with several other New Testament epistles. For 2026-2027 they read the gospel of Luke along with 1-2 Timothy, Titus, Philemon, James, 1-2 Peter, 1-3 John, and Jude. Using their study Bibles and other resources, students encounter scripture and seek to understand the cultural, historical, and theological context of their readings in order to more deeply appreciate the richness of the Bible and the humility needed in order to live out God's word today.

About Church History: Grades 10-12
Using a survey of key events in the American church, articles, and extra resources on Christianity across the world in the lesson plans, this course gives students a personal and big picture look at church history during the 19th century. A second text is used to bring the people and events of the early church to life.

About Spiritual Formation: Grades 10-11
Spiritual formation courses cover particular topics in the Christian life to encourage and deepen students' faith. This course focuses on reading, understanding, and following God's Word along with looking at how the things of this world point us to God.

entries.title: Placement & Combining Tips
entries.content:
New Testament: Grades 10-11
Lessons in one of the gospels are shared across HS grades. The epistles scheduled in Grades 10-11 are a little more thematically challenging than those in Grade 9, but lessons may be combined with Grade 9 or Grade 12 if desired. Students new to reading the Bible for themselves or unused to commentaries or study notes may find the lessons in Grades 7-8 a gentler introduction to these study practices.

Spiritual Formation: Grades 10-11
Can be combined with Grade 12 students. Students new to spiritual formation topics should begin with Spiritual Formation: Grades 7-8 or Bible History in Grade 9.

blocks.text: Take a moment to recall what you read previously in the same book or topic.

Read the passage assigned or read for the number of minutes allocated. If a book is challenging for you, try stopping to narrate to yourself silently now and then to make sure you are tracking. Or you could listen to an audiobook version of the text and read along. You may also want to read aloud to yourself. If a book is an easy read, enjoy the ride! Keep an eye out for quotes to put in your Commonplace Book or events or characters to put down in your Book of Centuries.

Process and integrate your reading by narrating either orally or in writing. Some lesson plans will give you a suggestion of how best to narrate. You might want to set a timer to keep yourself on track, so you stop reading with enough time to narrate.

Read notes in your Study Bible or commentary. Look up places mentioned in the text in your Bible atlas. Add something to your Book of Centuries or Commonplace Book. Jot down additional reflections and thoughts in your Bible notebook or share with others.

Read over the lesson plans to familiarize yourself with the content for accountability and interest. If doing the lessons together, use a picture, a map, or an engaging question to rouse students' interest before reading. You may also highlight one or two unfamiliar terms if needed. If in a classroom context, you may choose to leave larger doctrinal conversations for students to have with their parents at home.

lessons.title: 30m Old Testament: Grades 7-12 - Lesson 1
lessons.body:
⍞ Materials: Old Testament Today, notebook
➜ INTRO (For Grades 7-12)
This year you will be reading 2 Samuel, I Kings, I & 2 Chronicles, selections of Psalms, Proverbs and Ecclesiastes. You will be reading the passages chronologically or in the order of the events. "The goal of studying the Bible chronologically is not to replace the time-honored canonical arrangement, but instead to honor time as a setting in which the biblical record appeared. Readers who study the Bible this way will return to their traditional Bibles better equipped to read them. Acquiring a sense of the flow of the Bible's story will put flesh and blood on its message" (from Chronological Guide to the Bible).
1. Your ESV Concise Study Bible (Grades 7-8) or Cultural Backgrounds 
Study Bible (Grade 9-12) will have maps, articles, and notes to help you
along the way. If you have a question about a specific verse, see if there is
a note about that verse below the text.
2. Use your Student Bible Atlas or atlas in your Bible to locate places as
needed.
3. We will also utilize Old Testament Today and the Bible Project website 
as a handbook for helping us understand the background information we 
need in order to be informed readers.

➜ VIEW
1. Throughout the year, you will watch short videos of Dr. John Walton, the
co-author of OT Today. The videos were created specifically for Alveary
students. We hope they help you understand the Bible better. 
2. You will be reading selections from the Psalms throughout the year. Watch this video to learn more about the Psalms. 
∞ Video Link: Psalms

➜ READ, NARRATE & RESPOND
The psalms are difficult to tie to a specific historical context. They are the worship songs of Israel and are intended to apply to the needs of all worshippers across time. Some psalms do have historical superscriptions that make it possible to associate them with specific events, but these make up only about 10% of all the psalms. Another criterion is to place the psalms ascribed to David (or Solomon or Moses) during the life of that person. A second criterion is to place pslams according to their type (from Chronological Guide to the Bible)>
Psalms 79-82

lessons.title: 30m New Testament: Grades 10-11 - Lesson 1
lessons.body:
➜ INTRO
Each year in HS we will be reading one of the Gospels along with our study of the rest of the NT books. This year we will be reading Matthew along with James, Philemon, 1,2 Timothy, Titus, 1,2 Peter, Jude, and 1,2,3 John. It is helpful to be continually reminded of who Jesus is and how he interacted with people. The Gospel accounts were designed to be read many times.  There will be insights gained that you would only pick up after multiple readings and by paying attention to repeated words and themes. Alongside our study of Matthew, we will read the First Nations Version of Matthew.  Normally, when people translate the Bible for another culture, the text is not in English.  In this case, the text is in English, so we can join in!  Reading Matthew through the eyes of First Nations people helps us to see the gospel stories from a different perspective. It also gives us insights into the culture of the First Nations people. If you find you do not have time in the lesson for this reading, consider using it as your devotional reading.

We will make use of the Cultural Backgrounds Study Bible notes, the atlas, and some Bible Project videos/articles/podcasts to help us understand what Matthew and Paul are saying to their audiences.  Then we can think about what new thoughts about God we gain in the process.  You do not need to read all the study notes for all the verses.  Pick and choose based on which verses you have questions about. Use a digital or physical notebook to record your thoughts as you work your way through the readings. 

➜ VIEW & NARRATE
Today we will set the stage for reading Matthew by gaining some context. Narrate silently in your head or orally to yourself by going over the main points. Or, jot them down in your journal.
∞ Video Link: How to Read the New Testament
∞ Video Link: How to Read the Gospel
∞ Video Link: Matthew 1-13 Summary

➜ PRINT
You may want to print out the summary of the book to reference as you read even though it includes the second part of Matthew that we will watch when we get to those chapters.
∞ Image Link: Summary of Matthew Poster

➜ MAPWORK
When places are named in the text refer to a map in the study Bible or use your atlas.  The maps in the atlas that relate to Matthew are found on p.303-333.  Finding the places on the map is very helpful for seeing the stories in the context of the land.  Sketching maps of journeys or regions can be helpful too. The atlas will always be mentioned in the materials so that you always have it nearby.

lessons.title: 30m Spiritual Formation: Grades 10-11 - Lesson 1
lessons.body:
Reading the Bible for a Change
⍞ Materials: Reading the Bible for a Change

➜ INTRO
If I were to ask how you approach reading the Bible, what would you say? Do you have a color-coded system, dozens of notes written in the margins, and your name proudly stamped in the front of your Bible? Or are the pages of your Bible untouched? Perhaps you write everything down in a notebook? Or perhaps the Bible sits on your shelf and remains untouched. Whatever your answer, I invite you to put aside your pre-conceived notions about Bible reading and listen as Ray Lubeck invites you to a new way of reading the Bible. 

➜ ANNOTATE
Reading the Bible for a Change is a non-fiction book, which makes it a good candidate for annotation. While you are reading through the text, you may encounter an interesting point or main idea that you want to underline. You could put a question mark next to a statement you find unclear. You may place sticky notes on a page that you want to return to. Experiment with different ways to engage with the information in the book. Some students may record themselves reading or talking about the reading. Others may start a discussion with an acquaintance about the material. Try different ways and see what works for you. You can discuss your findings with your teacher.

➜ READ
Prologue p.1-5

➜ NARRATE
Take a moment and summarize the main ideas of this section either silently or aloud.

➜ WRITE
On page 3, Ray lists a couple of questions that have stayed with him for many years. Choose two of the questions and try to answer them in two paragraphs. You may want to refer back to these questions and your answers from time to time. 

➜ PONDER
In the prologue of Reading the Bible for a Change, Ray Lubeck tells the story of how someone misused the Bible when he was very little. He also tells the reader about the effects that this misuse had upon him. Think back to a time when the Bible was misused either by you or someone around you. Did you ever confront the person? How should we handle these misuses of the Bible?
lessons.teacherNotes:

Reading as Following 
⍞ Materials: Reading the Bible for a Change

➜ INTRO
Why read the Bible? What does it tell us? What are we supposed to take away from our reading? These are just some of the questions that Ray approaches in the first chapter. Think on these questions and don't be afraid to write down any that come to mind as you read. 

➜ ANNOTATE
Don't forget to annotate as you read.

➜ READ
Ch.1 p.9-17

➜ NARRATE
Take a moment and summarize the main ideas of this section either silently or aloud.

➜ WRITE
Ray Lubeck lists three hermeneutical virtues on pages 14-15. In one paragraph, describe how you see these virtues in your own reading of the Bible.

The First Step: Seeing
⍞ Materials: Reading the Bible for a Change

➜ INTRO
Ray Lubeck opens up chapter four by saying, "The first step to take in following the Bible is learning to see it carefully. Of course, I realize that anyone who has read the Bible has already 'seen' it—the words on the page are seen. But I have more in mind than this." What do you think he has in mind? What do you think "seeing" means when it comes to reading the Bible?

➜ ANNOTATE
Don't forget to annotate as you read.

➜ READ
Ch.4 p.50-56

➜ NARRATE
Take a moment and summarize the main ideas of this section either silently or aloud.

➜ PONDER

lessons.title: 30m Spiritual Formation: Grades 10-11 - Lesson 13
lessons.body:
The Third Step: Connecting continued...
⍞ Materials: Reading the Bible for a Change

➜ RECAP
Think back to last week's reading. What do you remember? What stood out to you?

➜ READ
Ch.6 p.105-116 "Following the Bible" through the end of the chapter.

➜ ANNOTATE
Reading the Bible for a Change is a non-fiction book, which makes it a good candidate for annotation. While you are reading through the text, you may encounter an interesting point or main idea that you want to underline. You could put a question mark next to a statement you find unclear. You may place sticky notes on a page that you want to return to. Experiment with different ways to engage with the information in the book. Some students may record themselves reading or talking about the reading. Others may start a discussion with an acquaintance about the material. Try different ways and see what works for you. You can discuss your findings with your teacher.

➜ NARRATE
Take a moment and summarize the main ideas of this section either silently or aloud.

➜ WRITE
At the end of this chapter, Ray Lubeck writes, "As you can see, life itself is a story to be lived. The Bible is not intended to be part of my story, but the other way around—my story is subsumed by the world-story that the Bible presents. Life makes sense as I align myself with the one meta-story. When I receive it as a comprehensive story, I understand the parts more clearly: both the parts of the Bible and all the parts of my life." Respond to the quote in 1-2 paragraphs. Do you agree or disagree? Why or why not? How does this statement inform your reading of the Bible?

➜ PONDER
What are the ideas from this section that you are taking away? What are you applying to your own Bible reading?

lessons.title: 30m Spiritual Formation: Grades 10-11 - Lesson 14
lessons.body:
The Fourth Step: Responding
⍞ Materials: Reading the Bible for a Change

➜ INTRO
Have you ever had the experience of reading the Bible and then asking, "So what?" If you have, you're not alone. And the fourth step, responding, will help you answer this question. As always, remember to take note of any questions that your reading brings up. You can make a physical note of these to make it easier to remember.

➜ READ & NARRATE
Ch.7 p.117-127 end at "into personal autobiography."

➜ ANNOTATE
Don't forget to annotate as you read. 

➜ WRITE
Ray Lubeck writes, "In the courses that I teach on Bible study, I have given assignments that required the students to list specific response suggestions that relate the shared Truth claims they have discovered in the text to their own lives. In observing their tendencies, I have been struck by two things: most of their suggestions have to do with outward actions rather than attitudes and perspectives, and they lack this element of imagination." Respond to this statement in one paragraph.

lessons.body:
How to Read Narrative
⍞ Materials: Reading the Bible for a Change

➜ INTRO
Are you a fan of narratives? Perhaps you like reading stories like The Lord of the Rings or Narnia? Or maybe you like to read biographical material about the lives of people you respect or who stir your curiosity. How about movies like Star Wars? Did you know that your brain is hardwired for these narratives? The Bible is overflowing with the genre of narrative, but that means we need to read it a certain way. As you spend time in this next chapter, take note of ideas that stand out to you. 

➜ READ & NARRATE
Ch.8 p.139-150 stop at "the entire story."

In last week's reading, Ray Lubeck wrote, "Every narrative has a setting, the environment in which the action takes place. It also includes characters, who are the participants in the actions. And it develops a plot, a series of events that are linked together. In addition to these three, all narratives employ elements of style or technique. Whenever we are reading a biblical narrative, we should look for how these elements are presented by the author." lessons.body:
Biblical Genres
⍞ Materials: Reading the Bible for a Change

➜ INTRO
You've learned about Narrative, Poetry, and Discourse, which are the predominant genres in the Scriptures. Now, Ray Lubeck turns to other biblical genres and how to read through them. From Apocalyptic, epistle, and gospel, to prophecy, proverbial, and Psalm, you'll learn how to navigate the other genres. Additionally, if you have any questions, I encourage you to write them down so you can refer back to them.

➜ READ & NARRATE
Ch.11 p.193-212

➜ ANNOTATE
Don't forget to annotate as you read.

➜ PONDER
Which of the genres listed in the chapter are you most familiar with? Do you tend to gravitate towards one genre or another? Why or why not?

lessons.title: 30m Spiritual Formation: Grades 10-11 - Lesson 25
lessons.body:
⍞ Materials: God of All Things

➜ INTRO
This is a book all about things.  But the things can point us to God.  So, really, this is a book all about things and God and how we can be reminded of him when we see and interact with the things of this world.  There are new wonders to see all around us! 

➜ ANNOTATE
Reading the Bible for a Change is a non-fiction book, which makes it a good candidate for annotation. While you are reading through the text, you may encounter an interesting point or main idea that you want to underline. You could put a question mark next to a statement you find unclear. You may place sticky notes on a page that you want to return to. Experiment with different ways to engage with the information in the book. Some students may record themselves reading or talking about the reading. Others may start a discussion with an acquaintance about the material. Try different ways and see what works and what doesn’t work for you. You can discuss your findings with your teacher.
 
➜ READ & NARRATE
Foreword, Introduction, Ch.1-2
Maybe a chart, diagram, or drawing for some of the chapters would be a good way to narrate.  Or, maybe writing a poem or a song about each item might make sense to capture the truths of the chapter.  Try a haiku with 5,7, and 5 syllables for each line. See what works well for you.

Ch.26 The Voice in the Voices, p.286-289 "Gospel of Thomas, were not."

➜ NARRATE
Take a moment and either silently or aloud summarize the main ideas of this section.

➜ DISCUSS
Read the following passage and explain what the author is communicating:
"What they passed on was not primarily a book, or a set of truths. It was a life. It was the truth...There are some things that you can only learn by absorbing them. You absorb such a thing by living with people who have it. And once absorbed, you can never fully articulate it. But you can live it, and you can pass it on to those you live with."

terms.content:
New Testament: Grades 10-11
· What was Matthew's emphasis in his gospel? What new thoughts did this reading provide about who Jesus is?

· What observations or insights did you gain from reading the First Nations Version?

Church History: Grades 10-12
· When the Church Was Young: Select an Early Church Father from the reading that you enjoyed learning about and identify several things you learned from him. Explain how you are incorporating those things into your daily living.

New Testament: Grades 10-11
· Give the substance and discuss the importance of the book of James OR 1 Timothy. 

· Give the substance and discuss the importance of Paul’s letter to Philemon. 

Church History: Grades 10-12
· When the Church Was Young: Write about the impact persecution had on the early church. How did the Early Church Fathers respond to the persecution they faced? Include examples.

New Testament: Grades 10-11
· Choose two to discuss:

1. A famous theologian said that “the Christian life begins with faith [which] results in spiritual growth [that] brings practical results” (2 Peter 1:1-11). Discuss. 

2. What does “abiding” entail as you understand it from 1-3 John? Any other passages?

3. Why would some theologians label Jude as “a call to arms”? How are believers to persevere?

4. How does Peter’s description of the church emphasize spiritual unity?

5. What does it mean to crave “pure spiritual milk”? Use scripture to support your answer.

Church History: Grades 10-12
· When the Church Was Young: Write about three important takeaways from the reading that have impacted your daily life and explain why.

Spiritual Formation: Grades 10-11
· Pick 4 things from the book and tell of their significance. Can you add one item that is not in the book from the world around you?  What lessons does it teach?
